subtopics.title: Meaning and Types of Values
common_misconceptions: Values are the same for all cultures
Only religious people have good values
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: The 1999 Constitution (Chapter 2, Section 23) specifies national ethics: Discipline, Integrity, Dignity of Labour, Social Justice, Religious Tolerance, Self-reliance, Patriotism. However, corruption, electoral malpractice, and ethnic conflicts show values gap. Teaching should connect values to everyday Nigerian life: queuing, obeying traffic rules, rejecting bribery, reporting corruption to EFCC/ICPC.

subtopics.title: Discipline
common_misconceptions: Indiscipline is only in young people
subtopics.teaching_note: Discipline = ability to do what is right even when no one is watching. Aspects: self-discipline (control of one's actions), obedience (following rules), punctuality (being on time), perseverance (not giving up). Consequences of indiscipline in Nigeria: traffic chaos (disobeying traffic lights), corruption (disobeying laws), examination malpractice (cheating in WAEC/NECO), cultism in universities. Benefits: personal success, national development, peaceful coexistence.

subtopics.title: Meaning and Types of Citizenship
common_misconceptions: Anyone born in Nigeria is a citizen (not always true)
Permanent residents have the same rights as citizens
Dual citizenship is illegal for all Nigerians
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian Constitution: Citizenship by birth (if born in Nigeria before independence with Nigerian ancestor; OR born after independence with Nigerian parent/grandparent). By registration (foreign women married to Nigerian men). By naturalization (15+ years residence, good character, acceptable livelihood). Dual citizenship: allowed for citizens by birth; NOT for naturalized citizens.

subtopics.title: Duties and Responsibilities of Citizens
common_misconceptions: Citizens only have rights, not duties
Tax evasion is a victimless crime
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian citizen duties (Constitution): obey laws, respect rights of others, promote national unity, render national service (NYSC for graduates), protect public property, pay taxes (FIRS), defend Nigeria. NYSC: one-year mandatory service for graduates under 30, established 1973. Voting: INEC conducts elections. Taxation: PAYE (Pay As You Earn), VAT (Value Added Tax 7.5%), company income tax.

subtopics.title: Meaning and Types of Human Rights
common_misconceptions: Human rights are only for citizens
Rights are absolute (they can be limited by law)
Freedom of speech means saying anything
subtopics.teaching_note: Sources: UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights (1981), Nigerian Constitution Chapter IV (1999). Types: Civil/political (life, liberty, fair trial, vote), Economic/social (work, education, health), Cultural (language, religion, customs). National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) Nigeria: investigates human rights violations. Limitations: rights can be restricted for public safety, order, morality, health - but must be 'reasonably justifiable in a democratic society.'
